speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE SOUNDING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is December 10, 1996. The survivor is Tom Lantos. The interviewer, Renee Firestone. We're in San Mateo, California, United States of America. And the language of the interview is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is December 10, 1996. It is my privilege this morning interviewing Congressman Tom Lantos. My name is Renee Firestone, R-E-N-E-E F-I-R-E-S-T-O-N-E. We are in San Mateo, California in the United States and we will speak English. Good morning, Congressman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Renee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for inviting us into your office and permitting us--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to tape your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we begin by telling us your name? Spell it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Tom Lantos, L-A-N-T-O-S. In Hungarian, it's Lantostamas L-A-N-T-O-S-T-A-M-A-S. And I was born in Budapest, Hungary on February 1, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In August of 1947. I actually arrived on August 23, 1947 in New York. I, I left Hungary on the 8th of August in a second-class railroad compartment. I was fortunate enough to get a B'nai B'rith Hillel Foundation scholarship to come to the United States to the University of Washington. At that time, I pronounced the town 'Seetle,' because nobody told me differently, that it's Seattle. I had a train ticket to Cherbourg, France. I arrived at the port of Cherbourg. I found my ship, which was a converted troop ship, the SS Marine Falcon.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will get into this--

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in our later time. Right now I would like to know whether you have been known under any other name.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never had any foreign papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you raised in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about your childhood and about the city itself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was an only child. My mother was an extremely intelligent and well-educated woman, a teacher. And my father was an intellectual and a banker. I came fairly late in life, and I, I was a very precocious child. I, I, I read at a very early age. I learned to play bridge at a, at a preposterously early age. And I had to sit in my parents lap, who held the 13 cards because my hands were too small to hold them, but I directed the play. I learned to play chess early on. I traveled with my parents. We were not wealthy. We were middle class, maybe upper-middle class. But I was very conscious of the, of the political climate, the economic climate from a very, very early age. And, and I-- and I was a very old young boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pal and Anna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the most precious memory you have of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's difficult. He was a very, very intelligent person, perhaps not as warm as, as my mother. I suspect it was learning a lot of things from him. I had a much warmer and much closer relationship with my mother than, than with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you spent time with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I spent time with both, a lot of time with both. But I was very, very close to my mother's family. She had two sisters, one brother. And it was an enormously tightly-knit family. And later on, as things developed, my-- I had three cousins, all men. And my mother's younger brother were the age where they sent these young men in labor battalions to the Russian front. All four of them perished, and I do not know how, or where, or when. This was in the early '40s. I was not part of this process because I was too young, but obviously, it had a tremendous impact on our family. As a matter of fact, my oldest cousin was a truly brilliant man called Dr. George Krileshein, a professor and a linguist whose specialty was Italian, married a lady literally weeks before he was sent to the Russian front as a Jew in a labor battalion. This lady still lives. I have supported her through the decades, and I see her every time I go to Hungary. A most remarkable woman who has been a widow since the early 1940s. It's now almost 1997. And while she's obviously very near the end of her life, throughout she has maintained her intellectual curiosity and interest. Every year for years I gave her a gift of a trip to anywhere she wanted to go to. And she had the most bizarre requests. She once wanted to go to Turkey, and I arranged for her a comprehensive bus tour of Turkey. She visited my wife and our children and me here in California several times. But it was a-- it was a very unusual family situation because of all of the quote unquote children in the extended family. My uncle was not yet married when he was taken to the Russian front and perished. And my three cousins, all of whom I was very close to, and I was exceptionally close to my uncle, they all perished. So the only human being in my family of my generation who survived was me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back for a minute. What schooling did you have in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended elementary school in my neighborhood called Sziget Utcai Elementary School. I spent four years there. Then I enrolled in a gymnasium, a high school, by the name of [NON-ENGLISH] Gymnasium. I was fortunate enough to be in an exceptionally strong class. Of course, it was a non-coeducational school. All the schools at that time were non-coeducational. It was-- it was-- It still exists. It was located and is located on a street called Marko Utca. Across the street was the ambulance service of Budapest. So all day we heard the ambulances come and go. I was a very good student, but there were lots of very good students in that class. Probably the best is still my, my closest friend, with whom I went through school, went through forced labor camp, and he now is a very distinguished physician in Canada. We just had one of our many class reunions in Budapest in September, just two and a half months ago. My education, of course, was interrupted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go into that, was the school a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school was a public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any interactions, any friendships, with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I had-- I had several good friends who were non-Jewish, one of them by the name of Romai Lazslo. He was a Catholic boy and we visited back and forth. We were very good friends. I had a very good friend who is still alive and I maintain contact with him. He lives in Brussels. He was half Jewish. His name was Halasz Ivan. And his father took him and me out on many excursions, and we have remained close friends. The school was attended by a very large number of Jewish boys, but it was-- but it was mixed and we had-- we had interaction. I also have very mixed memories of the teachers. The principle teacher of my class was a wonderful, wonderful gentleman called Horvath Antal. Horvath means Croatian in Hungarian. And he was-- he had a special designation, as people did who distinguished themselves in the first World War. The special designation was the word [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means courageous. So his name was [NON-ENGLISH] Horvath Antal, courageous Anthony Horvath. And I have a marvelous-- he was, among others, a Latin teacher. And I never forget the amount of Latin we memorized daily. We would march through the school room chanting prepositions that took the accusative and prepositions that took the ablative in Latin. And I can still do it. [LATIN]. And you can wake me up in the middle of the night. I learned this when I was 10. In, in six weeks I'll be 69. But we learned it well. We learned the ones that take the ablative. We learned to conjugate and decline. [LATIN]. We learned Hungarian poetry. I can't tell you how many lines. Unlimited numbers of lines, and I remember them all. And while I think, you know, in the broader scheme of things it was an idiotic waste of one's time, in a sense, it, it enriched our lives. And I find it very interesting, Renee, that of course, with my children and grandchildren they are living and growing up under totally different circumstances. But growing up in a small country gives you a very different outlook on life than growing up in a very large country. I learned every tiny rivulet in Hungary, and every tiny village, and every, every aspect of that history, which, by the way, just celebrated it's 1,100 anniversary. I was, I was at a, at a fascinating concert just a few weeks ago, sitting in the Presidential box with the Secretary of Defense of the United States, the American Ambassador to Hungary, the Hungarian Ambassador to, to Washington, and the Deputy Secretary of State, my friend Strobe Talbott and our wives celebrating the 1,100 anniversary of the birth of Hungary. I can draw you the map of Hungary in excruciating detail, in excruciating detail. And I must say that since some of my very happiest experiences relate to Hungary, and of course, some of the most horrendous experiences of my life relate to Hungary, this, this dichotomy would have been conducive to the development of a schizophrenic personality, because I, I love the place, and I love the music, and I love the literature. I love the food. I love the wine. I love the music, the dances, the theater. But I have a passionate hatred for, for the fascists and, and the people who were such a nightmare.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child, did you encounter any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot. A lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I come from a very assimilated family. I had Jewish education as an assimilated boy in Budapest would have. I was bar mitzvahed in the large synagogue. And by the way, I was there now for the official reopening of the large synagogue a few months ago in September with my friend, former Prime Minister Shamir of Israel, and my friend, an enormously decent human being, the President of Hungary, President Goncz. And my friend, the Israeli Ambassador to Hungary, translated Prime Minister Shamir's speech. The Israeli Ambassador is one of the Mengele twins. So you, you, you get chills and goose pimples as you-- as you think of these relationships and contacts. Yes, I had-- I had fistfights. And I had-- the phrase [NON-ENGLISH], rotten Jew, was often used. I, I was a very feisty boy, and I fought back. And I, I had a lot of physical encounters. Some I won, some I lost. I was very conscious of the fact that Jews were being squeezed commercially, educationally, culturally, in every sense. As you know, the first anti-Jewish law after the First World War restricting the number of Jews attending university. Numerus clausus, was passed in Hungary, not in Germany. I was-- I was a very political boy from early on. My parents were very political. My uncle was very political. So I read the newspapers and I, I knew all these fascist, semi-fascist, anti-Semitic, right-right political leaders. They, they were part of my upbringing. I can sing you some bawdy songs about these various fascist bastards who my parents taught me to hate. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I, I remember the first newspaper I bought, I bought, was coming back from school the day Hitler marched into Austria, the day of the Anschluss. And the newspapers, in banner headlines, said, Hitler marches into Austria. And I was coming back from school, and I was 10 years old. But I sensed that this is a historic day. So for the first time in my life, I took out my little pouch and took the few pennies that I had and bought that newspaper and, and took it home, because I knew I was bringing home an important piece of news.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way did your family observe the Jewish religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were not kosher. We observed the high holidays. I was bar mitzvahed. We celebrated Hanukkah. We fasted on Yom Kippur. But I, I felt very Jewish in a, in a cultural and political sense. I-- Jews come in many sizes and shapes, and, and there was a lot of discussion about converting and so on and so forth. And I never entertained that for a moment. I mean, I was very proud to be a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean why the family did not leave?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- did they encounter-- did they think about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, in retrospect not leaving appears like an idiotic decision. And since in '38, '39 I was 10, 11 years old, I, I have difficulty putting myself in the boots of, of my elders. But I believe that the main reason was that the enormity of the Holocaust that was to follow could not be conceived. I mean, we, we, we-- you know, we listened to the BBC and we listened to, to Western broadcast. And we understood that a war is coming and so on. But the expectation was that we would survive the war, that Hungary, under Admiral Horthy, was an ally of Germany, and it will be difficult for the Jews. But it, it never crossed anyone's mind in our family. Obviously, it was a fatal flaw. The Jews would be exterminated. I mean, we-- you know, we knew the history of the persecution of the Jews through the centuries, but even in Spain in 1492, it was an expulsion and not, not a Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you bought the newspaper, what did you read? What did you understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I not only understood the basic facts that Germany took over Austria, but I also understood that many of the Austrians loved it, and, and that the, the German troops were received. And subsequently, of course, I have seen motion pictures of this with tremendous adulation, and excitement, and joy. The Austrians, with great skill over the years, have attempted to paint Austria as the first victim of Hitler, when in point of fact, to an overwhelming extent, they were the first co-conspirator with Hitler. I, I don't think I was sophisticated enough to analyze it. But perhaps subliminally, I felt that while Austria had been a separate country, it was nevertheless a German speaking country. And there was some, some difference between Nazi Germany taking over another German country, German-speaking country, and Hungary, which was very different, linguistically, historically, culturally. And-- but I-- but I'm reluctant to analyze because I think I'm analyzing from the retrospective knowledge of, of my having lived through these last 68, 69 years, rather than the way I looked at it as a boy of 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first hear the, the name Hitler, and what did it mean to you? How did you understand who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my family talked about it in the early '30s. And I listened to-- I spoke German. As a matter of fact, I first learned German before I learned Hungarian. And I was, I no longer am, but I was practically bilingual in the late-- in the '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you speak to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke Hungarian to my parents, but both of my parents were absolutely fluent in German. My mother also was fluent in French and English. And, and I, I heard Hitler on the radio. I can't give you the year, but I was a little boy. Whether I was five or seven, I couldn't tell you, but it was at about that stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you began to comprehend what the word Hitler meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very difficult one to answer because you see, even after the Anschluss, the, the taking of Austria, I did not fully understand what the word Hitler and Nazism meant. I understood the hatred for the Jews. I understood the persecution of the Jews. I understood Kristallnacht. I understood lots of things, but I, I really was part of and, and born into a highly civilized Western European family and culture. And gas chambers were not part of my, of my, of my scheme or of my framework. I, I knew, for instance, that there was such an organization as Hitlerjugend. An aunt and uncle of mine had a villa up in Buda, and a very large place very near it was taken over for the summer by Hitlerjugend, in the early '40s. I don't remember what year. And I saw these German, young boys playing, and exercising, and, and, and being there. But I really did not, even at that time, could go down the road all the way to what the Holocaust would mean. I, I knew it was a, a horrible, vicious, anti-Semitic, anti-democratic, rotten crowd. But I did not dream that would-- it would-- it could go this far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you realize that you were excluded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. I very much realized it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I resented it, but I was always very self confident and, and I did not feel inferior. I felt excluded. I felt discriminated against, but I never felt, never for a moment, did I feel inferior.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you fear? Were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were situations, there were situations, which were frightening. Jews being beaten up. And of course, to me, personally, the most direct impact was the shipping of my uncle and cousins in labor battalions to the Soviet front where, as old Jews, they were not allowed to bear arms, but they did the dirty work of the Hungarian Second Army-- digging trenches, and clearing minefields, and working like pack horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will stop for a moment to change tapes and we'll go into this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good.
